one_line_takeaway: A SoK that unifies the fragmented LLM-guardrail landscape into a 6-dimension taxonomy and a Security-Efficiency-Utility benchmark, empirically showing no guardrail wins on all axes and that today's defenses collapse against adaptive multi-turn attacks (>90% ASR on X-Teaming).
key_finding: No guardrail dominates all SEU dimensions -- security, efficiency, and utility trade off against each other. On Llama-3-8B-Instruct, GuardReasoner (Pre) gives the strongest average defense (mean ASR 0.135) but at high latency and GPU cost, while SmoothLLM is weakest (mean ASR 0.303). Reasoning-based LLM guardrails buy accuracy with the largest delay and memory footprint. Two systemic weaknesses stand out: (1) current session-level guardrails fail badly on adaptive multi-turn attacks -- most exceed 90% ASR against X-Teaming (GradientCuff is the best exception at only 64%) and stay above 10% ASR against ActorAttack; and (2) LLM-based guardrails generalize poorly to prompt-injection attacks (non-trivial but modest filtering), though injections did not hijack the guardrails' core safety function. Structural findings: post-processing variants always add more latency than the same model's pre-processing variant (must await generation); session-level (Post) guardrails achieve markedly lower FPR than their sequence-level (Pre) counterparts (e.g. WildGuard Post <5% vs WildGuard Pre >10% on OR-Bench).
core_contribution: A Systematization-of-Knowledge (SoK) paper on jailbreak guardrails (external defense modules that monitor/filter LLM interactions without touching model weights). Its own three contributions are: (1) a multi-dimensional taxonomy classifying guardrails along six dimensions -- Intervention Stage (pre/intra/post-processing), Technical Paradigm (rule/model/LLM-based), Safety Granularity (token/sequence/session-level), Reactiveness (static vs dynamic), Applicability (white- vs black-box), and Explainability -- with 40+ prior guardrails catalogued in Table 1; (2) a Security-Efficiency-Utility (SEU) evaluation framework with concrete metrics (ASR, PGR for security; Extra Delay and GPU-memory overhead for efficiency; FPR for utility); and (3) a unified empirical benchmark/leaderboard applying SEU to nine existing guardrails against nine attacks. Note: the individual guardrails (Llama Guard, WildGuard, GuardReasoner, etc.) and attacks (GCG, AutoDAN, X-Teaming, etc.) are surveyed/re-implemented prior work, NOT the authors' own inventions -- the contribution is the taxonomy, the SEU framework, and the comparative measurement.
threat_model: Jailbreak attacks: an adversary crafts a prompt P to elicit a response R = LLM(P) that satisfies a harmful objective G (JUDGE(R,G)=True), bypassing the model's safety alignment. Formalized as maximizing Pr[JUDGE(R,G)=True]. With a guardrail GR present, an attack succeeds only if BOTH the LLM's alignment AND the guardrail are circumvented -- i.e. Assess(GR, X)=allow (on prompt P, internal feature F, or output R) while JUDGE(R,G)=True. Attack surface spans single-turn (manual, optimization-based, generation-based, implicit/multilingual) and multi-turn jailbreaks, plus a cross-attack probe of prompt-injection robustness against the LLM-based guardrails themselves.
limitations: Efficiency numbers (latency, memory) are hardware-specific (NVIDIA H800) and would shift with different hardware or quantization, though the paper argues relative trends hold. Jailbreak-success scoring depends on an LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o), a known reliability risk, mitigated but not eliminated by the human-agreement check on 100 samples. Reviewer-side gaps: main quantitative tables center on Llama-3-8B-Instruct (other models deferred to appendices); the prompt-injection generalization test uses a single 203-sample dataset (deepset/prompt-injections); the taxonomy's latter three dimensions (Reactiveness, Applicability, Explainability) are treated qualitatively and not measured; and the study covers only text jailbreaks, with multimodal guardrails left to future work.

datasets_used: JailbreakHub (IJP) -- 1000 in-the-wild manual jailbreak prompts sampled
JailbreakBench -- 100 harmful instructions driving GCG, AutoDAN, TAP, LLM-Fuzzer, DrAttack, X-Teaming
deepset/prompt-injections -- 203 injection samples for cross-attack test
Target LLMs: Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Vicuna-13b-v1.5, GPT-4-0125-Preview; GPT-4o as judge
quantitative_results: Best average defense: GuardReasoner (Pre) mean ASR 0.135 on Llama-3-8B-Instruct
Worst average defense: SmoothLLM mean ASR 0.303
Undefended Llama-3-8B-Instruct baseline average ASR 0.238 (0.910 on X-Teaming)
Multi-turn failure: most guardrails >90% ASR vs X-Teaming; GradientCuff best exception at 64% (0.640); session-level guardrails >10% ASR vs ActorAttack
Highest FPR: SelfDefend (Direct) 0.221 on OR-Bench; GradientCuff 0.083 highest on AlpacaEval
Utility by granularity: WildGuard (Post) FPR <5% vs WildGuard (Pre) >10% on OR-Bench
Prompt-injection PGR (higher = more injections pass): Llama Guard(Pre) 0.911, SelfDefend(intent) 0.749, GuardReasoner(Pre) 0.680, WildGuard(Pre) 0.655, SelfDefend(direct) 0.512
Leaderboard: PromptGuard highest composite score (but low M-PGR); SelfDefend(Intent) most balanced (weakness = FPR)
LLM-as-judge validation: 0.9533 human agreement, Fleiss' Kappa 0.9319 on 100 samples
